data.title: Trump Proposes New EU Tariffs, Sending Futures Lower and Gold Higher
data.description:
Global markets moved into a defensive posture as US and European equity futures declined following President Donald Trump's announcement of proposed tariffs on European countries opposing his plans to acquire Greenland. Nasdaq 100 (QQQ, Financial) futures fell 1.2% while European futures slid about 1%, reflecting renewed sensitivity to trade risk after an extended AI-led rally. Asian equities edged lower overall, though declines were measured and pockets of resilience emerged, including gains in South Korea. In currencies and commodities, the dollar weakened against most major peers as investors rotated toward haven assets, with gold and silver extending their rally to record highs alongside gains in US Treasury futures and core European bonds, even as US cash Treasury trading is closed for a holiday.

Trump said the US will impose a 10% tariff starting Feb. 1 on goods from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the UK, the Netherlands and Finland, with rates set to rise to 25% in June unless a deal is reached for what he described as the complete purchase of Greenland. The announcement prompted swift pushback from European leaders and raised the possibility that the EU could suspend approval of a trade agreement concluded last year, while France may seek activation of the bloc's anti-coercion instrument. Strategists suggest the renewed tariff threat introduces a fresh layer of uncertainty for markets that had been supported by resilient corporate earnings and sustained AI-related investment, with analysts cautioning that an escalation into a broader EU-US trade conflict would likely carry negative consequences on both sides.

In Asia, strategists noted that regional equities appear relatively resilient compared with US and European peers, supported by ongoing optimism around AI investment trends and China's reported 5% GDP growth meeting official targets. Meanwhile, the euro reversed early losses to trade higher, possibly reflecting speculation that European governments could reassess exposure to US assets amid trade tensions, while cryptocurrencies sold off in line with the broader risk-off tone. Some market participants argue that the initial “Sell America” reaction could prove temporary, with expectations that Trump's tariff threats may function as a negotiating tactic, leaving room for a rebound should tensions ease in the days ahead.

data.title: $7 Trillion in China Deposits Near Maturity as Investors Seek Higher Returns
data.description:
Chinese households are beginning to reassess long-held cash positions as roughly $7 trillion in time deposits mature this year, a buildup that traces back to a prolonged property downturn and years of muted equity returns. With deposit rates now drifting toward 1% following multiple cuts since 2021, savers appear increasingly motivated to explore alternatives beyond traditional bank products. Market participants suggest this evolving behavior could gradually redirect capital toward equities, wealth management products, and insurance, a shift that aligns with Beijing's objective of encouraging steadier capital market participation to support broader economic momentum.

Encouraged by a rebound in equities that has added more than $1 trillion in market value over the past month, some households are cautiously increasing exposure to stocks, particularly in technology-linked segments. Chinese equities have trended higher since April despite periods of global tariff uncertainty, while the Star 50 Index has climbed more than 12% in 2026, underscoring renewed interest in growth-oriented names. At the same time, gold prices have reached record highs, with domestic investors contributing to the rally, while demand for participating insurance policies has strengthened as investors seek relatively stable returns amid a low-rate environment.

From a policy perspective, authorities appear focused on guiding this capital reallocation in a measured way. Officials have reiterated support for a so-called “slow bull market,” aiming to promote sustainable wealth creation while avoiding sharp boom-and-bust cycles seen in prior years. After major benchmarks reached multi-year highs in 2025 and extended gains into 2026, regulators moved to tighten oversight of margin financing and curb overly aggressive market promotion. For many savers, this has translated into a more balanced approach, with funds flowing into insurance and diversified products that retain some equity exposure, potentially allowing household capital to remain within the financial system while gradually adjusting risk profiles.

data.title: Micron Warns AI Demand Is Overwhelming Supply
data.description:
Micron Technology (MU, Financial) is saying the quiet part out loud. The memory-chip crunch driven by AI demand is not easing anytime soon and management now expects it to stretch beyond 2026.

“The shortage we're seeing is truly unprecedented,” Micron operations chief Manish Bhatia told Bloomberg, pointing to how high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerators is soaking up industry capacity. That pull from AI is so strong it is creating knock-on shortages for traditional markets like PCs and smartphones, forcing device makers to lock in memory supply years in advance.

Demand from hyperscalers and customers such as Nvidia (NVDA, Financial) has reshaped the memory landscape. Micron says its AI-focused memory products are already fully booked through 2026. Rivals SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics saw similar tailwinds last year, with SK Hynix noting its entire 2026 output is sold out. Micron's decision to exit its Crucial consumer brand late last year underscored how aggressively it is prioritizing enterprise demand.

tight supply gives memory leaders pricing power and locks AI infrastructure in as the industry's main growth engine. Micron is now betting big on capacity, including a $1.8 billion Taiwan fab deal and roughly $200 billion in U.S. expansion, with meaningful new output expected from 2027.

data.title: Trump Denies Offering Fed Chair Job to Dimon
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM, Financial) CEO Jamie Dimon was never considered for the top job at the Federal Reserve, President Donald Trump said Saturday, directly disputing a Wall Street Journal report that suggested otherwise.

The Journal reported earlier this week that Trump had floated the idea of Dimon becoming Fed chair during a White House meeting several months ago and that Dimon dismissed it as a joke. Trump responded bluntly on social media, calling the report “totally untrue” and saying, “Why would I give it to Jamie?” He insisted there was never any offer.

Trump also escalated tensions with JPMorgan Chase & Co., saying he plans to sue the bank “over the next two weeks” for what he described as improperly debanking him after the Jan. 6 attack on the U.S. Capitol. JPMorgan has not publicly responded to the threat.

The comments came shortly after Jamie Dimon criticized Justice Department subpoenas involving renovations at the Federal Reserve, adding another layer of friction between Wall Street and Washington.

the episode highlights how quickly politics can spill into banking, keeping JPMorgan's leadership under an uncomfortable spotlight. Investors will be watching whether Trump's legal threat gains traction or fades as another political flare-up.

data.title:
Gold, Silver Hit Records as Trump Greenland Tariffs Rattle Markets
        (Status: Pending)
data.description:
Gold and silver continued their powerful advance as investors reassessed geopolitical and policy risks following President Donald Trump's escalating push to take control of Greenland. Spot gold traded near $4,670 an ounce, while silver rose as much as 4.4%, moves that coincided with a softer US dollar and growing concern that tensions between the US and Europe could spill into a broader trade conflict. The Trump administration plans to impose tariffs on eight European countries, including France, Germany and the UK, starting with 10% levies on Feb. 1 that are scheduled to increase to 25% in June, targeting nations opposing the Greenland initiative.

European officials are now weighing potential responses, with leaders expected to convene an emergency meeting in the coming days to discuss countermeasures. Options under consideration reportedly include retaliatory tariffs on €93 billion of US goods, while French President Emmanuel Macron may seek to activate the European Union's anti-coercion instrument, which would allow the bloc to deploy a range of measures in response to what it views as coercive trade actions. Market participants view the Greenland-related dispute as distinct from last year's Liberation Day tariffs, as it could signal a deeper geopolitical rift within long-standing alliances, possibly embedding a more persistent risk premium across global markets.

The rally in precious metals has been reinforced by a broader mix of macro and geopolitical factors. Gold and silver have already posted sharp gains following strong advances in 2025, supported by concerns around US foreign policy, renewed pressure on the Federal Reserve, and questions over central bank independence that have encouraged investors to seek alternatives to currencies and government bonds. Demand from China has added momentum, while gold-backed exchange-traded funds increased holdings by 28 tons last week, the largest weekly inflow since September. As spot gold climbed 1.6% to $4,670.47 an ounce in Singapore trading and silver rose to around $93.18, investors are also closely watching an upcoming US Supreme Court hearing on Trump's effort to remove Fed governor Lisa Cook, which could prove important for perceptions of the Fed's independence.

data.title: NVIDIA (NVDA) Faces Competition in AI Market as Analysts See Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Chip giant NVIDIA (NVDA, Financial) is facing challenges in maintaining its status as the prime investment choice in the AI boom. RBC Capital Markets analyst Srini Pajjuri views this as an ideal opportunity to invest in NVIDIA. Despite benefiting from TSMC's strong earnings report, NVIDIA's prominence in the AI investment narrative is waning, with attention shifting to memory-related sectors.

Market concerns revolve around NVIDIA potentially losing market share to Google's in-house TPU and the upcoming chips from Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). NVIDIA's stock has shown a consolidating trend over the past three months. However, Pajjuri is optimistic about NVIDIA regaining its growth momentum, initiating coverage with an "outperform" rating and a target price of $240.

Pajjuri cites robust capital expenditure among major cloud service providers as a contributory factor. Although the AI accelerator market share for NVIDIA might decline from 80% to 70% by 2027, he doesn't perceive this as significant. While custom ASIC chips and AMD's products could gain share due to supply chain diversification, NVIDIA's performance lead and comprehensive end-to-end solutions mitigate these risks. NVIDIA's Rubin system production and future flexibility with Groq enhance its competitive edge.

This stock alert was generated using automated technology and GuruFocus financial data to provide readers with timely and accurate market reporting. data.title: Micron Stock Rises. A Director Just Made a $7.8 Million Bet
data.description:
Micron (MU, Financial) added to the positive tone after director Teyin Liu disclosed a purchase of 23,200 shares, a transaction worth about $7.8 million.

Shares of MU rose after 1% in after hour trading on Thursday.

Liu bought at prices between $336.63 and $337.50 and now directly owns 25,910 shares, SEC filings show.

The size and price range of Liu's purchases, at roughly $337 per share, underscores a sizable personal stake.

Insider buying can signal confidence, traders said, though company fundamentals and broader demand trends remain the decisive factors.

Analysts said upcoming earnings and memory-price trends will be key to sustaining the rally.

Investors will watch guidance, capex and memory pricing for clearer demand signals.

For now, the chip sector's short-term lift looks tied to analyst calls and insider buying, while near-term cyclical demand risk still persists.

data.title: TSM Exceeds Expectations with Strong Growth Prospects and AI Demand
data.description:
Citigroup's research report highlights that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM, Financial) significantly surpassed its previous guidance and market expectations in its latest quarterly performance. TSM achieved a gross margin of 62% and an operating profit margin of 54%, with a net profit of 505.7 billion New Taiwan dollars, exceeding forecasts by 10%.

The report notes that the demand for advanced manufacturing processes, especially in artificial intelligence, will drive TSM's annual revenue growth close to 30%. TSM has updated its long-term annual compound growth rate from 20% to 25%. Additionally, the company's capital expenditure for this year is projected to be between $52 billion and $56 billion, which is significantly higher than anticipated.

Citigroup believes the tight supply in advanced semiconductor manufacturing will persist. TSM remains a focus stock with a "buy" rating and has had its price target raised from 2,450 New Taiwan dollars to 2,600 New Taiwan dollars.

This stock alert was generated using automated technology and GuruFocus financial data to provide readers with timely and accurate market reporting. data.title: Amazon Unveils Europe Cloud to Boost Data Security
data.description:
Amazon (AMZN, Financials) launched its first Europe-based cloud service designed to give customers more control over their data and reduce dependence on U.S.-operated systems.

The new AWS European Sovereign Cloud will store data only in Europe, where European laws apply, and will work autonomously from Amazon's U.S. infrastructure. The executives claimed that the move makes sure that data would still be available even if connections across the Atlantic are cut off or export restrictions are put in place.

The first data center is being built in Brandenburg, Germany, and more will be built in other parts of Europe. The €7.8 billion investment will help run activities run by a German company that is made up completely of EU nationals. This meets tight government and commercial criteria for handling sensitive data.

The launch comes at a time when more and more people want to protect their data and their rights after worries about U.S. legislation that let the government see information collected by American digital companies. Microsoft and Google, two of Amazon's competitors, have done similar things to get European customers by giving localized data storage and compliance alternatives.

The new service gives Amazon a chance to grow its presence in the European cloud industry, where trust, legislation, and data control are now important considerations in competition.

data.title: Microsoft Strikes Record Deal for U.S. Soil Carbon Credits
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT, Financials) has signed a record agreement to purchase 2.85 million soil carbon credits from Indigo Carbon, reinforcing its commitment to achieve carbon negativity by 2030.

The 12-year contract marks the largest-ever deal for soil-based carbon removal, with an estimated value of $171 million to $228 million. The credits are tied to regenerative farming practices in the United States that enhance soil's ability to absorb and store carbon.

Microsoft, already the world's biggest buyer of carbon removal credits, said the partnership with Indigo aligns with its sustainability strategy to offset emissions from its rapidly growing network of AI-powered data centers. The company said it was drawn to Indigo's verified approach and its direct financial benefits to farmers, who will receive 75% of the revenue from each credit issued.

Soil carbon credits reward agricultural methods such as reduced tilling, cover cropping, and rotational grazing techniques that help restore soil health while capturing atmospheric carbon.

Industry observers said the deal signals a growing corporate shift toward “high-integrity” carbon removal projects. However, some environmental experts caution that measuring and maintaining long-term soil carbon storage remains a challenge.
